moveAnnotations.annotation: We're stepping into the Polish Opening, an unusual first move that immediately signals we want to control the long diagonal and prepare rapid queenside expansion. While unconventional, b4 stakes a claim on c5 and supports future moves like Bb2, giving us a flexible setup that avoids the main-line theory that most players study. The move suits aggressive, creative players who prefer fighting on their own terms rather than entering heavily analyzed positions.

Black chooses a solid, restrained response, preparing to develop the kingside while keeping central tension flexible. By playing e6 instead of immediately challenging b4, Black allows us to continue our queenside initiative but maintains the option to strike in the center later with moves like d5 or c5. This move is characteristic of French-like setups where Black prioritizes solid structure over rapid piece development.

Now we fulfill the main idea of b4—developing our bishop to the long diagonal where it exerts pressure on e5, f4, and importantly, supports our expansion. Bb2 is flexible and doesn't commit us to a specific pawn structure; we can later push b5 or reroute if needed. This bishop placement is the thematic feature of the Polish Opening, giving us long-range influence while keeping our position coordinated.

Black develops actively, attacking e4 and preparing to support the center or kingside. Nf6 is a developing choice that doesn't commit Black to a specific plan yet, keeping options open for both central breaks (like d5) and kingside castling. The knight on f6 is well-placed to support future play on any part of the board.

We push forward aggressively, attacking Black's space and forcing a decision about how to handle our advanced pawns. By playing b5, we're committing to queenside pressure and preparing to create weaknesses on Black's queenside; the move also supports a potential c4 advance later. This is the critical moment where we're claiming space and not allowing Black to sit comfortably.

Black attacks our b5 pawn, forcing us to make a critical choice about our pawn structure. Rather than retreat, a6 invites us to capture, which will lead to the unusual Rooks Swap line where material gets traded off rapidly. This move shows Black playing actively to simplify the position and reduce our attacking potential.
